title: Setting up whole-home energy monitoring
subtitle: Clamp monitor, smart plugs, and a dashboard that shows where the energy goes
excerpt: A whole-home energy monitor gives a real-time view of total consumption and identifies when large appliances are running. Combined with individual smart plugs, it shows exactly where the electricity bill comes from.
sourceNotes: Energy Saving Trust home energy monitoring guidance. OFGEM consumer energy monitoring guide.

projectSchedule.title: Review your first week of baseline data
projectSchedule.body: After seven days of monitoring, review the daily consumption pattern. Identify the largest loads and note which appliances account for what share of daily use. This is the baseline against which you will measure any changes.

projectSchedule.title: Check your first monthly total
projectSchedule.body: Compare the monitored total with your electricity bill for the same period. If the figures differ by more than 5 percent, check whether the clamp is on the correct cable or whether any circuits bypass the monitored cable.

content.text: Most households have no real-time view of their electricity use. A smart meter IHD shows how much power is being drawn right now, but it does not log history or show which appliances are responsible. A whole-home clamp monitor and smart plugs fill that gap: they show what is running now, what ran overnight, and which appliances drive the monthly bill.

A current-transformer clamp fits around the live feed cable inside the consumer unit. It reads the magnetic field generated by the current flowing through the cable and calculates instantaneous power in watts. The clamp connects by wire or wirelessly to a display unit or a hub that feeds data to an app. Installation does not require isolating any circuits or cutting the cable: the clamp clips onto the outside of the cable insulation.

The consumer unit contains live mains voltages. The clamp goes on the tails between the meter and the consumer unit, not inside the consumer unit itself. If the meter cupboard is sealed or the tails are inaccessible, ask your installer to fit the clamp.

Clamp energy monitor: a wireless current-transformer clamp and a hub or display unit. Most current models connect to a home wifi network and log data to an app. The clamp itself fits cables up to 16 mm diameter for single-phase domestic supply.

Smart plugs with power monitoring: individual plugs for major appliances (washing machine, dishwasher, tumble dryer, EV charger, electric heaters). These report per-appliance consumption to the same app.
A smartphone or tablet for the monitoring app.
Locate the meter tails: the two thick cables running from the electricity meter to the main switch on the consumer unit. These are typically 10-25 mm diameter twin-core cables.

Clip the clamp around the live (usually brown or red) tail only. Do not clip both tails together: the magnetic fields cancel and the reading will be zero. Clip the neutral separately if a second clamp is required for an unbalanced three-phase supply (rare in domestic settings).
Connect the clamp cable to the transmitter unit. Position the transmitter within wireless range of the hub. Most systems have a range of 10-20 m through walls.
Configure the hub and app. Enter the current electricity unit rate in the app so the display converts kWh to cost. The unit rate appears on your electricity bill.
Verify the reading. Turn on a known appliance (a 2 kW kettle) and confirm the monitor shows approximately 2,000 W increase. This confirms the clamp is reading the correct cable.

A whole-home monitor running for two weeks typically reveals the always-on consumption (the baseline when all active appliances are off), which in a typical UK home is 200-500 W continuously. Over a year, 300 W always-on uses 2,600 kWh. At 27p/kWh that is over 700 pounds per year in standby and background loads alone.

attrs.symptom: Monitor reads half the actual consumption
attrs.cause: Clamp is fitted to a neutral rather than a live tail
attrs.fix: Refit the clamp to the live (brown) tail. The live and neutral carry the same current in a single-phase supply, but the orientation of the clamp on the neutral may read in reverse; some models show half or zero for this reason.

attrs.cause: Wireless interference between the transmitter and the hub
attrs.fix: Move the hub closer to the meter, or use a wired connection if the monitor supports it. Avoid positioning the transmitter near a wifi router or other RF devices.
